title: A Distributed Switching Protocol for Quantum Networks
authors: Aman Yacob Tekleab, Yifeng Shen, Yoshii Yutaro, Amin Taherkhani, Rodney Van Meter, Shota Nagayama
abstract: With the advent of the construction and deployment of entanglement-based quantum networks, the efficient use of network resources will become a critical challenge for the scalable operation of such a system. Recently, architectures that incorporate memoryless optical switches have gained attention for forwarding entangled photons. By leveraging these architectures, costly resources such as high efficiency Bell State Analyzers (BSAs) can be shared across the network. Nevertheless, the introduction of switching substantially complicates the process of multiplexing and resource allocation compared to an individual link. In this work, we propose a switching protocol for unbuffered, multidrop quantum networks in a photonic synchronization domain that establishes a link between two end nodes using a shared BSA in the switched network. To achieve this, two end nodes cooperatively select the target BSA node with the lowest path cost and independently reserve each path within the network. Bi-path reservations are performed to allocate resources in a distributed manner. The proposed protocol is evaluated through simulation on Q-Fly network topologies under varying traffic conditions. The results demonstrate high link establishment success with stable performance even under increased network load. These capabilities which are driven by our proposed protocol are an essential way to realize large-scale, managed, and automated quantum networks.
summary: This paper proposes a new distributed protocol for managing resources in quantum networks that use shared optical switches. By allowing nodes to independently reserve paths to a shared Bell State Analyzer, the protocol enables scalable and automated entanglement-based networking. Simulations show that the protocol remains effective even as network traffic increases.
main_problem: The challenge of efficient resource allocation and multiplexing in unbuffered, multidrop quantum networks that utilize shared optical switches and Bell State Analyzers (BSAs).
main_result: The proposed distributed switching protocol achieves high link establishment success and maintains stable performance even under increased network traffic loads.
method: A distributed protocol where end nodes cooperatively select the lowest-cost BSA node and perform independent bi-path reservations to allocate network resources.
model_or_system: Unbuffered, multidrop quantum networks in a photonic synchronization domain, specifically evaluated using Q-Fly network topologies.
key_observables: Link establishment success rate and network performance under varying traffic loads.
important_parameters: Network traffic load and path cost.
main_assumptions: The network uses memoryless optical switches and operates within a photonic synchronization domain.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of resource allocation in switched quantum networks, proposes a distributed switching protocol based on bi-path reservations, and validates the protocol through simulations on Q-Fly topologies under different traffic conditions.
